summary: Charity Majors post-mortems the backlash to her advice post telling women in tech to stay technical, earn power, and then use it to advocate for others. She walks through what feedback was valid (sloppy framing around gender identity, insufficient defensive writing) versus what was bad-faith pile-on, ultimately standing by her core advice while acknowledging specific communication failures.
key_points: Her original advice: tough it out as a junior, stay technical, then use your senior power to advocate for others
Valid criticism included not writing defensively for hostile readers and being sloppy about her own gender identity framing
She distinguishes between genuine feedback from trusted friends and performative outrage from internet mobs
The private response was overwhelmingly positive (100-1), especially from junior women, while public discourse was dominated by criticism
She frames the tension as between people who fight to succeed within a flawed system versus those who refuse to participate in it
money_quotes: We are far too chickenshit about telling young women straight up how to succeed at work. We praise them for all kinds of dumb shit and second shift work and emotional labor that has little if any strategic impact to the bottom line, and wonder why they're burned out and resentful.
I have no problem admitting my errors and making amends. I do it all the fucking time. But I am disinclined to grovel before a howling mob.
I'm tired of seeing women burn themselves out on shitty corporate diversity work that only diverts their energy from amassing real power and strategic impact.
I don't want a fucking consolation prize. I want to sweep the competition, I want to change the world. I can't be the only one who hungers for power, money and credibility.

There has always been tension between the people who see the world as it is and fight to succeed in it, and the people who opt out and refuse to participate because it's compromised. The world needs us both.
key_insight: Honest, strategic career advice for women in tech — stay technical, accumulate real power, then use it — is more empowering than the corporate diversity messaging that diverts energy from building actual influence.
